extraction.summary: On 2025-11-25, the Story County (IA) Board of Supervisors approved Resolution #26-39, a one-time extension of Resolution #25-97 continuing a temporary moratorium in unincorporated Story County on accepting applications and issuing permits for digital asset mining/cryptocurrency operations, data processing centers, and battery/energy storage solutions through 2026-03-31.
extraction.notes: Extraction is from Board minutes summarizing Resolution #26-39; the full resolution text (findings, exemptions, authority, and exact permit types) is not included, so several fields are unknown or inferred from the title language.

definition.text: Temporary moratorium on the acceptance of applications and/or issuance of permits for Digital Asset Mining, Cryptocurrency Operations, Data Processing Centers and/or Battery/Energy Storage Solutions on properties located in unincorporated Story County.

findings.key_concerns_text: County staff has compiled draft regulations that are going through an internal review currently... awarded technical assistance... to assist with the development of the regulations.

prohibition.geographic_detail: properties located in unincorporated Story County
prohibition.key_text: temporary moratorium on the acceptance of applications and/or issuance of permits for Digital Asset Mining, Cryptocurrency Operations, Data Processing Centers and/or Battery/Energy Storage Solutions

study_process.assigned_departments: Planning and Development Department; County Attorney’s Office (review)
study_process.key_text: County staff has compiled draft regulations... anticipate the draft regulations will be forwarded to the County Attorney’s Office for review in early Dec

extraction.summary: Story County Board of Supervisors (IA) considered Resolution #26-39 to extend Resolution #25-97’s temporary moratorium in unincorporated Story County on accepting applications and issuing permits for digital asset/cryptocurrency mining, data processing centers, and battery/energy storage facilities. The extension is described as a one-time extension through March 31, 2026 while staff finalize draft regulations with technical assistance.
extraction.notes: Extraction is based on agenda text and a staff memo; the full text of Resolution #26-39 and Resolution #25-97 is not included, so authority citations, exact suspended approval types, effective/adoption dates, exemptions, and standard clauses may be incomplete.

extraction.summary: The provided text appears to be a Sierra Club Lower Hudson Group newsletter (Terra Firma) discussing environmental advocacy topics (EPA endangerment finding, plastics, water, data centers) and includes an internal Sierra Club election ballot. It is not a local government moratorium ordinance/resolution and contains no operative land-use permit suspension provisions to extract.
extraction.notes: Document is advocacy/newsletter content, not a governmental moratorium instrument. If a moratorium is referenced elsewhere in the PDF, provide the relevant ordinance/resolution text for extraction.

extraction.adopting_body: President of the United States (Trump Administration)

findings.key_concerns_text: “excessive state regulation” creates a patchwork of compliance regimes, may “embed ideological bias” in models, and attempts to regulate beyond State borders.

prohibition.geographic_detail: Nationwide federal policy aimed at preempting state AI laws (not a land-use permitting moratorium).

exemptions.key_text: Legislative recommendation “shall not propose preempting otherwise lawful State AI laws” relating to child safety protections; AI compute and data center infrastructure (other than generally applicable permitting reforms); State procurement/use of AI; and other topics as determined.

study_process.assigned_departments: Department of Commerce; DOJ (Litigation Task Force); FCC; FTC; other agencies
study_process.key_text: Secretary of Commerce shall, within 90 days, publish an evaluation of existing State AI laws identifying “onerous” laws and those to be referred to the DOJ Task Force.

extraction.summary: This document is a private law-firm client alert summarizing a purported 2025 federal Executive Order, “Ensuring a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence,” directing DOJ/Commerce/FCC/FTC to pursue preemption of state AI laws and to condition certain discretionary federal grants (including BEAD) on states not enacting/enforcing “burdensome” AI laws. It is not a local land-use moratorium and does not suspend zoning/building approvals.
extraction.notes: Not an actual ordinance/resolution text; no EO number, signature date, or operative legal clauses provided. Treated as federal executive action summary; moratorium taxonomy fields largely not applicable.

findings.key_concerns_text: extends the moratorium for four months which will allow the village time to amend the zoning ordinance as well as a permanent ordinance for commercial solar energy and wind energy systems

prohibition.key_text: A Resolution Approving a Moratorium on Commercial Solar Energy Systems and Wind Energy Systems

study_process.key_text: allow the village time to amend the zoning ordinance as well as a permanent ordinance for commercial solar energy and wind energy systems for the village

extraction.summary: The Diamond Village Board approved an amendment to Resolution No. 2024-04 extending a moratorium on commercial solar energy systems and wind energy systems. The minutes state the extension lasts four months to allow the Village to amend its zoning ordinance and prepare permanent regulations. The underlying moratorium terms, suspended approval types, definitions, exemptions, and detailed findings are not included in the meeting minutes.
extraction.notes: Extraction is based on meeting minutes rather than the full resolution/amendment; operative moratorium clauses are mostly not provided.

extraction.authority_citation: K.S.A. 12-741, et seq., and K.S.A. 19-101, et seq.

definition.text: Data Centers shall be generally construed as one or more buildings, facilities or other sites that are constructed, reconstructed, enlarged, remodeled, leased or used to principally house a group of networked computer servers for the storage, processing, management and dissemination of data and information, or a substantially similar use.

findings.key_concerns_text: due to a number of impacts that Data Centers have or might have on both nearby property owners, the region, and also future development... could be detrimental to the health, safety, and general public welfare

prohibition.geographic_detail: unincorporated area of Sedgwick County
prohibition.key_text: officials... are directed to suspend the receipt, review, hearing, or granting of such applications, approval, licenses, permits, or authorization for the period stated within this Resolution.

duration.early_termination_text: may be extended, amended, or terminated earlier than its expiration date by subsequent resolution approved by the Board of County Commissioners

exemptions.key_text: Conditional Use Permits or Building Permits for applications submitted after this Resolution’s effective date that would include or involve Data Centers is temporarily suspended

study_process.assigned_departments: Metropolitan Area Planning Department (MAPD), Metropolitan Area Building and Construction Department (MABCD), and Wichita-Sedgwick County Metropolitan Area Planning Commission (MAPC)
study_process.key_text: MAPD staff and the Wichita-Sedgwick County Metropolitan Area Planning Commission... are directed to review the existing Zoning Code. The MAPC may then bring forward any proposed Zoning Code amendments pertaining to Data Centers

extraction.summary: Sedgwick County, Kansas adopted Resolution No. 014-2026 establishing an interim development control for data centers in the unincorporated county. It suspends receipt, review, hearing, consideration, and granting of conditional use permits, building permits, licenses, approvals, or other authorizations for post-effective-date data center applications until April 17, 2026, while MAPD/MAPC review zoning amendments.
extraction.notes: OCR has minor date/number artifacts; title and operative clauses clearly establish a temporary interim development control for data centers in unincorporated Sedgwick County.

prohibition.key_text: Data Center Moratorium conversation was held. Resolution to adopt the Ordinance Establishing Data Center Moratorium as presented was offered by Thomas Campbell and supported by Melissa Goschka.

extraction.summary: Oneida Charter Township's Board of Trustees voted 7-0 on January 13, 2026 to adopt an ordinance described in the minutes as an "Ordinance Establishing Data Center Moratorium." The provided board-packet OCR does not include the ordinance text, so the moratorium's operative prohibitions, duration, findings, exemptions, and definitions cannot be determined from this document.
extraction.notes: The packet text contains meeting minutes noting adoption of an Ordinance Establishing Data Center Moratorium, but the ordinance text itself is not included in the provided OCR excerpt.

findings.key_concerns_text: A medium-sized data center consumes as much water as 1000 households per year for cooling (110 million gallons). Rapid and improperly regulated data center construction poses a potentially serious threat to our natural water resources.

prohibition.key_text: Our preference in this moment of uncertainty would be to pause all new data center construction until we can develop appropriate legislative mechanisms for managing its downsides.

study_process.assigned_departments: Wisconsin Legislature
study_process.key_text: Legislators’ recently proposed moratorium on data center construction should be used to give the Legislature time to create thoughtful controls that ensure new data centers actually benefit Wisconsinites.

extraction.summary: River Alliance of Wisconsin submitted testimony to a Wisconsin legislative committee on SB 729, SB 843, and AB 840 concerning data center development. The document is not an enacted moratorium, but it supports pausing all new data center construction while the Legislature develops controls, emphasizing water consumption, hydropower impacts, and utility cost concerns.
extraction.notes: This is advocacy testimony/letter from River Alliance of Wisconsin regarding SB 729, SB 843, and AB 840, not an enacted moratorium instrument. It supports a proposed pause on new data center construction.

extraction.summary: The provided PDF text appears to be a Pennsylvania DEP media/news clips digest dated March 27, 2026, not a moratorium ordinance, resolution, or governmental action. It lists links to news stories, including several about data centers and one environmental group call for a pause, but contains no operative moratorium language, adopting jurisdiction, findings, duration, or permit suspension provisions.
extraction.notes: Non-moratorium news digest; extraction limited to confirming absence of ordinance/resolution content in provided text.

findings.key_concerns_text: residents asked the Board to put a moratorium in place until zoning ordinances and public policy were better established to ensure that local control could be maintained

study_process.key_text: until zoning ordinances and public policy were better established to ensure that local control could be maintained

extraction.vote_result: 3-2 (Folk, Guenthner, and Voigt aye; Wolf and Bauman nay)
extraction.summary: The Mercer County Commission adopted by motion a 1-year moratorium on data centers being permitted in Mercer County, ND. The action followed resident concerns about a proposed NextEra Energy Resources and Basin Electric Cooperative data center project and a request to pause permitting until zoning ordinances and public policy were better established. The minutes do not specify exemptions, waiver procedures, or detailed permit-processing coverage.
extraction.notes: Source is approved meeting minutes rather than a standalone ordinance/resolution. Motion states only a 1-year moratorium on data centers being permitted; details on acceptance, processing, exemptions, or definitions are not provided.

findings.key_concerns_text: Presentation materials describe data centers as factories with major energy consumption, diesel emergency generators, transmission-grid access needs, noise over 90 dB, water for cooling, AI-related e-waste, copper demand, and resource competition.

extraction.summary: This PDF is a presentation titled “Datacenter Degrowth and Decentralization as a Chance for Europe,” not an enacted moratorium ordinance or resolution. It discusses data-center energy demand, water use, diesel backup generators, noise, e-waste, copper demand, and resource competition, and contains a slide reference to “Moratorium!” but no operative legal prohibition, duration, exemptions, or adopting governmental body.
extraction.notes: Not a legal moratorium instrument; appears to be conference/presentation slides by Thomas Fricke dated November 14, 2025. Extraction reflects absence of ordinance terms.

extraction.adopting_body: Council of the Village of Lordstown
extraction.authority_citation: Constitution of the State of Ohio and the Ohio Revised Code; Ohio Revised Code Section 121.22

definition.text: “Data Center” means a facility, used for the storage, management, and dissemination of digital data, which houses computer, telecommunication, and/or network equipment, systems, servers, appliances, and other associated components related to digital data operations, and/or facility designed or modified to house networked computers and data and transaction processing equipment.

findings.key_concerns_text: data centers are industrial-scale facilities that consume significant energy, water. and infrastructure capacity, creating water supply issues, significant utility demands that the Village may be unable to support, potential adverse impacts to the environment and Village residents.

prohibition.geographic_detail: within the corporate limits / within the Village of Lordstown
prohibition.key_text: temporary moratorium not to exceed one-hundred-and-eighty (180) days, on the granting/issuing of any new permits, as well as the receipt and processing of applications for permits, under Local Codified Ordinances, including Chapters 1115, 1125, and/or 1135

exemptions.key_text: Municipal or emergency service data infrastructure operated by the Village of Lordstown or its authorized agents. Small-scale server rooms incidental to permitted business operations, provided they do not exceed 500 square feet and are not the primary use of the property.

study_process.assigned_departments: Council, committees, Planning Commission, and/or the Village of Lordstown Planning and Zoning Department
study_process.key_text: undertake a review and/or study of the Ordinances of the Village of Lordstown... and perform further due diligence on Data Centers to determine how Data Centers will impact the Village’s infrastructure, utilities, environment, residents, and long-term planning goals

extraction.summary: The Council of the Village of Lordstown, Ohio adopted Ordinance No. 1-2026 imposing a temporary moratorium for up to 180 days on new permits and receipt/processing of permit applications for data centers within the Village. The ordinance defines data centers functionally, tolls permit and site plan review deadlines, provides limited exemptions for municipal/emergency service infrastructure, small incidental server rooms, manufacturing/showrooms, and declares an emergency.
extraction.notes: OCR contains minor typographical errors. Section 2 says Council 'recommends' a moratorium, but Sections 7 and title impose it. Expiration calculated as 180 days from Jan. 9, 2026; document states not to exceed 180 days from effective date.

extraction.summary: This is not an enacted moratorium instrument; it is a March 5, 2026 NYSDEC/NYSRC meeting summary describing proposed New York State legislation, S.9144/A.10141, that would prohibit data centers from receiving permits. The described moratorium would last until 90 days after DEC regulations and PSC orders are completed, with DEC and PSC action barred before three years after enactment, and would involve a DEC/NYISO GEIS on electricity consumption and interconnection-queue impacts.
extraction.notes: Extraction is from a meeting-summary description of a pending state bill, not the bill text or an adopted moratorium; many ordinance-style clauses are absent or unknown.

extraction.summary: This NYSDEC/NYSRC meeting summary describes proposed New York State legislation, S.9144/A.10141, that would impose a moratorium on data center permit issuance. The moratorium would last until 90 days after DEC regulations and PSC orders are completed, with those agency actions barred before three years after enactment/effective date. The proposal would require DEC, working with NYISO, to prepare a GEIS addressing data center electricity consumption and interconnection queue impacts.
extraction.notes: This is a meeting summary describing pending legislation, not the text of an enacted moratorium ordinance or resolution; extraction is limited to provisions summarized in the document.

findings.key_concerns_text: The brief argues that reversal or delay of the US Wind project would cause Maryland to lose over $6 billion in projected economic benefits, 1,710 megawatts of electricity, and avoided pollution benefits.

prohibition.geographic_detail: Not a state or local moratorium instrument; federal litigation brief concerning offshore wind permits.

extraction.summary: This document is an amicus brief filed by the State of Maryland in federal litigation, not a moratorium ordinance, resolution, or executive order. It supports US Wind's motion for a preliminary injunction and discusses economic, energy-grid, and environmental benefits of an offshore wind project, while referencing a federal presidential memorandum described as a moratorium on wind-related permitting activities.
extraction.notes: Non-moratorium litigation filing. The only moratorium reference is to a federal presidential memorandum targeting offshore wind leasing/permitting; this brief itself adopts no land-use moratorium.

definition.text: "Data center" means a facility used to house computer systems and associated components, including telecommunications and storage systems, where the primary function is the storage, management, and dissemination of data.

findings.key_concerns_text: The purpose of this Act is to ensure Illinoisans living on the Mahomet Aquifer have adequate access to water and to ensure transparency regarding the water usage of data centers operating in the State.

prohibition.geographic_detail: Statewide prohibition limited to data center use of water/groundwater withdrawals from the Mahomet Aquifer.
prohibition.key_text: No data center operating within this State shall use water from the Mahomet Aquifer. No State agency shall issue or renew a permit authorizing a data center to withdraw groundwater from the Mahomet Aquifer.

study_process.assigned_departments: Illinois Environmental Protection Agency
study_process.key_text: The Agency shall publish an annual report summarizing statewide water consumption trends in data centers, including, but not limited to, legislative recommendations to address identified issues.

extraction.summary: Illinois SB4004 would create the Data Center Water Transparency and Aquifer Protection Act. It prohibits data centers from using water from the Mahomet Aquifer beginning January 1, 2027 and bars State agencies from issuing or renewing related groundwater-withdrawal permits. Existing data centers must cease such withdrawals by January 1, 2028; the bill also voids local NDAs restricting disclosure of water-use information.
extraction.notes: Introduced Illinois state bill creating a permanent aquifer/water-use restriction and transparency regime, not a local moratorium ordinance or enacted document.

findings.key_concerns_text: Residents addressed council on concerns regarding proposed Data Centers, including long-term health effects, health risks, environmental impact, strain on utilities, constant noise, and limiting Data Centers to one area by the Casey Highway not in neighborhoods.

extraction.summary: Archbald Borough Council held a work session on November 12, 2025 at which residents raised concerns about proposed data centers, including health, environmental, utility, and noise impacts. The notes mention a resident proposal for a temporary moratorium and pending zoning applications for data centers, but they do not enact or extend any moratorium.
extraction.notes: This document is work session notes, not an enacted moratorium. It records public comments about proposed data centers and one resident raising the possibility of a temporary moratorium; no moratorium was adopted in the text.

extraction.summary: The supplied PDF text is not a land-use or infrastructure moratorium ordinance; it is a 2011 scientific article on snow crab distribution in Alaskan waters. The only moratorium-related language states that in 2009 the North Pacific Fishery Management Council recommended, and the U.S. Secretary of Commerce approved, a moratorium on Arctic fishing. No operative legal clauses, duration, exemptions, or permitting details are provided.

definition.text: "hyperscale data center" means a centralized repository for the storage, management, dissemination, and processing of electronic data and information which has two or more of the following: (1) A size exceeding two hundred fifty thousand square feet; (2) A peak electrical demand of fifty megawatts or greater; (3) An artificial intelligence model training or large-scale machine learning purpose; or (4) A co-location configuration with undisclosed end-users.
definition.size_threshold: two or more of: size exceeding 250,000 square feet; peak electrical demand of 50 MW or greater; AI model training or large-scale machine learning purpose; co-location with undisclosed end-users

prohibition.geographic_detail: in this state
prohibition.key_text: Notwithstanding any other provision of law, a moratorium is imposed on any new construction or expansion of a hyperscale data center in this state.

exemptions.key_text: The moratorium does not apply to any hyperscale data center that obtained all final government approval and required permits, and executed all utility interconnection agreements, before July 1, 2026.

extraction.summary: South Dakota Senate Bill 232, Amendment 232A, proposes a statewide one-year moratorium on new construction or expansion of hyperscale data centers. The moratorium would run through June 30, 2027 and exempts projects with all final government approvals, required permits, and utility interconnection agreements before July 1, 2026. The bill defines hyperscale data centers by data-processing function plus two or more size, power-demand, AI/machine-learning, or co-location characteristics.
extraction.notes: Text is an introduced state legislative bill amendment rather than evidence of an enacted moratorium; extraction treats the proposed Act's operative terms as written.

definition.text: which has two or more of the following: (1) A size exceeding two hundred fifty thousand square feet; ... (3) An artificial intelligence model training or large-scale machine learning purpose; or (4) A co-location configuration with undisclosed end-users
definition.size_threshold: size exceeding two hundred fifty thousand square feet; other threshold text partially shown as "greater"

extraction.summary: A City of Kings Mountain staff report asks City Council to approve a 182-day moratorium on permitting new data centers within the city’s jurisdiction. The report cites N.C.G.S. 160D-107(d), the absence of UDO definitions or standards, and concerns about location, buffering, lighting, noise, utility usage, and electrical grid limitations. The moratorium would run from February 24, 2026 to an August 25, 2026 public hearing while staff develops text amendments with the Planning and Zoning Board.
extraction.notes: Source text is a staff report for a public hearing, not the enacted ordinance or minutes; adoption and vote are not shown. Extraction treats the requested moratorium terms as operative but confidence is limited.

The resolution cites concerns about safe construction and operation, nearby property rights, and county road conditions.

extraction.summary: Wisconsin Assembly Bill 1099 would create Wis. Stat. § 134.44 imposing a statewide moratorium on operating qualifying data centers. The prohibition would continue until multiple statutory prerequisites are in place, including a statewide planning authority, customer cost-shift protections, subsidy elimination, water/electricity reporting, pollution safeguards, renewable-energy and wage requirements, NDA restrictions, local referendum approval, and enforcement provisions.

definition.text: Data centers have been around for over a decade, providing the digital services we use every day. But demand is rapidly increasing, and genAI is a key driver of that growth.

findings.key_concerns_text: Data centers have become a growing concern, as their water use, energy needs, and long-term impacts on local water supplies are often unfamiliar to communities, and difficult to assess without clear information and transparency.

extraction.summary: The City Council of Leavenworth, Washington adopted Ordinance No. 1561 as an emergency interim zoning control for cryptocurrency mining, effective immediately on March 27, 2018 for up to twelve months. It defines cryptocurrency mining, limits it to conditional-use approval in specified commercial and light industrial districts, prohibits it as a home occupation/accessory use, and requires study of electrical-grid and safety impacts with Chelan County PUD involvement.
extraction.notes: Interim official control primarily creates temporary zoning definitions/performance standards and limits cryptocurrency mining to conditional-use districts, rather than a blanket ban in every zone.

extraction.summary: Michigan House Bill 5882, introduced April 23, 2026, would create statewide rules for local moratoria on large-scale data centers and cryptocurrency mining facilities. extraction.summary: Minnesota H.F. 4888 is an introduced state bill that would bar the state and local governments from issuing permits, including conditional use permits, for data centers. The moratorium would last until one calendar year after the Public Utility Commission submits a comprehensive data center impacts report, due July 1, 2027 with possible extension to January 1, 2028. The bill requires analysis of energy, water, environmental, Tribal, land use, and economic impacts.
extraction.notes: This is an introduced Minnesota House bill, not an enacted moratorium document; dates depend on final enactment and PUC report submission.

extraction.summary: Ohio H.B. 710, as introduced in the 136th General Assembly, is a statewide bill that would prohibit public incentives for new data centers and limit where they may be built. It defines “new data center” functionally and excludes facilities already under construction or with final local/zoning/land-use approval; it also imposes prime farmland/public land/residential-neighborhood restrictions and utility cost-neutrality requirements before approval, permitting, or interconnection.
extraction.notes: As introduced bill, not an enacted local moratorium ordinance; no findings, adoption date, or sunset clause appear in the text. Extraction treats permanent proposed statutory restrictions as open-ended.

No. 710, as introduced in the 136th General Assembly, would prohibit public incentives for new data centers and limit their construction on prime farmland, public land, and land within platted residential neighborhoods. extraction.summary: Larimer County’s Board of County Commissioners adopted a resolution establishing a temporary moratorium on data center facilities in unincorporated Larimer County. It bars acceptance, pre-submittal activity, and processing of building, engineering, planning, and other development applications while Land Use Code standards are developed. It expires August 25, 2026 unless earlier ended by adoption of regulations or continued.
extraction.notes: Recorded April 9, 2026; signature/date OCR and stated August 25 expiration appear somewhat inconsistent with the six-month-from-effective-date language, so adoption/effective date is inferred from filename/signature OCR as April 7, 2026.

definition.text: The term “artificial intelligence data center” means a facility or connected facilities that are used for the development or operation of AI models at scale, or that have a total peak power load in excess of 20 megawatts and use either high-performance server racks or liquid cooling systems.
definition.size_threshold: total peak power load in excess of 20 megawatts

findings.key_concerns_text: New or upgraded AI data centers must not increase the electric and utility bills of consumers, harm the environment, or make climate change even worse; the American people must be empowered to approve or reject construction or upgrading in their local communities.

prohibition.geographic_detail: United States; also includes a separate restriction on export of U.S.-origin advanced AI chips and computing hardware to countries/entities lacking AI safety, worker, and environmental laws.
prohibition.key_text: A moratorium is hereby imposed on the construction or upgrading of all new AI data center projects until Congress passes comprehensive AI legislation that ensures the safety and prosperity of the American people.

duration.early_termination_text: This moratorium shall not be lifted until legislation is passed and such legislation expressly terminates the moratorium.

study_process.assigned_departments: Department of Energy
study_process.key_text: The Department of Energy shall collect and publish data related to AI data centers, including ... water usage; energy usage and infrastructure needs; ... emissions ... air quality ... jobs ... wastewater ... noise levels; certification of no ... subsidies.

extraction.summary: This section-by-section summary describes a proposed federal Artificial Intelligence Data Center Moratorium Act. It would impose an open-ended moratorium on construction or upgrading of new AI data center projects until Congress passes comprehensive AI legislation expressly terminating it, with DOE reporting and limits on subsidies, utility impacts, labor, and environmental harms.
extraction.notes: Source appears to be a legislative summary/proposal rather than enacted law; adoption date, vote, and formal bill number are not provided. State field set to US for federal jurisdiction.

extraction.instrument_number: S. [bill number not assigned in text]

definition.text: the term ‘‘artificial intelligence data center’’ means all the buildings, equipment, structures, and other stationary items, such as server racks, that— (1) are located on a single site or on contiguous, adjacent, or otherwise connected sites; (2) are owned or operated by the same entity...; and (3)(A) are used for the development or operation of artificial intelligence models at scale; or (B)(i) have a maximum rated power capacity or total peak power load in excess of 20 megawatts
definition.size_threshold: maximum rated power capacity or total peak power load in excess of 20 megawatts; designed/equipped to deliver 20 kW or more to a single server rack or use liquid cooling

findings.key_concerns_text: Mark Zuckerberg... is building a data center in the State of Louisiana that is the size of Manhattan and will use 3 times the quantity of electricity that the entire city of New Orleans uses every year

prohibition.geographic_detail: United States / federal scope; export-control provisions also apply to transfers to countries without comparable laws.
prohibition.key_text: Beginning on the date of enactment of this Act, the construction or upgrading of new or existing artificial intelligence data centers may not commence or proceed until— (1) 1 or more laws are enacted...; and (2) a provision... expressly terminates the moratorium

duration.early_termination_text: until 1 or more laws are enacted that satisfy section 3(b)(1) and a provision in those laws expressly terminates the moratorium

study_process.assigned_departments: Secretary of Energy / Department of Energy
study_process.key_text: The Secretary of Energy shall submit to Congress quarterly reports on artificial intelligence data centers and make those quarterly reports publicly available on the website of the Department of Energy.

extraction.summary: This 119th Congress Senate bill, titled the Artificial Intelligence Data Center Moratorium Act and introduced by Mr. Sanders, would impose a federal moratorium on construction or upgrading of AI data centers. It would continue until Congress enacts AI-safety and data-center-guardrail legislation expressly terminating it, and it adds DOE reporting and export-control provisions.

findings.key_concerns_text: The Wichita-Sedgwick County Unified Zoning Code does not include a specific definition for data centers. Therefore, such facilities are currently regulated on a case-by-case basis dependent on their characteristics and operations.

prohibition.geographic_detail: Especially within the unincorporated areas of Sedgwick County; the referenced interim control concerns County approvals.
prohibition.key_text: On January 14, 2026, the Sedgwick County Board of County Commissioners approved a 90-day interim control period on the approval of any zoning or building permits for data centers.

study_process.key_text: The purpose of the interim control is to allow time for research on best practices, the development of regulatory recommendations, and the establishment of development controls specific to data centers.

extraction.summary: This is a Berkley Group planning white paper prepared for the Wichita Metropolitan Area Planning Department, not the enacted interim-control instrument itself. It reports that the Sedgwick County Board of County Commissioners approved a 90-day interim control period on January 14, 2026, barring approval of zoning or building permits for data centers while MAPD and consultants study best practices and draft Unified Zoning Code amendments.
extraction.notes: Source is a planning report/white paper summarizing a moratorium, not the underlying county resolution or ordinance. It also states that on March 4, 2026 the Commission was considering extension through June 11, 2026, but does not itself enact that extension.

extraction.summary: This PDF is a July 20, 2010 research brief by Allison Plyer and Richard Campanella describing 2008 coastal Louisiana employment as a baseline for assessing Deepwater Horizon impacts. It is not a governmental moratorium instrument and contains no operative suspension of permits, approvals, or drilling authorizations.
extraction.notes: Document is a research brief on coastal employment before the 2010 Deepwater Horizon oil disaster, not an ordinance/resolution imposing a moratorium. It mentions a deepwater oil drilling moratorium only as economic context.

extraction.summary: The City of Tulsa City Council proposed an ordinance imposing a moratorium through December 31, 2026 on accepting, processing, or issuing building permits for data center use within Tulsa. It exempts Project Anthem Phase 1 and one additional Project Anthem Phase 2 data center, provides Council appeals/exemptions for good cause, requires 60-day planning reports, and ends earlier if amended regulations take effect.
extraction.notes: Text appears to be a draft/final mark-up with blanks for ordinance number, publication, and adoption dates; extraction based on available text.

extraction.summary: The Town of Mishicot adopted Resolution No. 2026-01 petitioning Manitowoc County to enact a one-year interim control ordinance for data centers not already approved. The requested moratorium would suspend acceptance, consideration, and issuance of permits, with a possible six-month extension, while the county studies impacts and develops standards.
extraction.notes: This resolution does not itself enact a moratorium; it petitions the Manitowoc County Board to adopt a county interim control ordinance for data centers.

definition.text: “data center” means a facility having a primary purpose of storing, managing, and processing digital data and that satisfies any of the following: (a) Has at least 5,000 servers. (b) Occupies at least 10,000 square feet. (c) Has an electricity demand of at least 100 megawatts.
definition.size_threshold: at least 5,000 servers; at least 10,000 square feet; or electricity demand of at least 100 megawatts

findings.key_concerns_text: prohibition on shifting data center energy and water costs to residential customers; mandatory data center public reporting of water and electricity usage; safeguards concerning air, water, and noise pollution

duration.early_termination_text: unless all of the following are provided by law, as determined by the secretary of agriculture, trade and consumer protection

exemptions.key_text: data center means a facility ... that satisfies any of the following: Has at least 5,000 servers; Occupies at least 10,000 square feet; Has an electricity demand of at least 100 megawatts.

extraction.summary: Wisconsin Senate Bill 1061 would create Wis. Stat. § 134.44 imposing a statewide moratorium on operation of large data centers. The bill bars operation unless numerous safeguards are enacted by law, including planning authority, cost-shift prohibitions, subsidy elimination, public water/electricity reporting, pollution safeguards, renewable-energy and wage requirements, NDA restrictions, and local referendum approval.
extraction.notes: This is an introduced state bill referred to committee, not an enacted ordinance or resolution; adoption/effective dates and vote are not stated. Moratorium targets operation rather than permit acceptance or issuance.

prohibition.geographic_detail: YCUA water and sewer service commitments within the Authority service area

extraction.summary: Ypsilanti Community Utilities Authority’s Board considered Resolution 26-08 on April 22, 2026, for a 12-month moratorium on water and sewer service commitments for hyperscale/mid-sized data centers and AI/HPC facilities. It bars will-serve letters, conditional approvals, capacity reservations, and infrastructure commitments pending due-diligence studies.
extraction.notes: Board packet memorandum describes a proposed resolution; full enacted resolution text and vote are not included. Prior related actions by Ypsilanti Township and City of Ypsilanti are referenced, but this is not an extension.

definition.text: hyper-scale data centers, mid-sized data centers, artificial intelligence computing facilities, and high-performance computational centers

findings.key_concerns_text: these centers increase water and demands, infrastructure strain, and operational costs ... may consume up to 5 million gallons of water per day ... generating significant wastewater discharges associated with cooling systems, chemical treatment, and thermal discharge

prohibition.geographic_detail: Ypsilanti Community Utilities Authority water and sewer service area/communities served by YCUA
prohibition.key_text: immediately impose a moratorium of not less than 12 months on the delivery, commitment, reservation, extension, or approval of water and sewer services to hyper-scale data centers, mid-sized data centers, artificial intelligence computing facilities, and high-performance computational centers

study_process.assigned_departments: Ypsilanti Community Utilities Authority; independent qualified third-party experts where appropriate
study_process.key_text: completion of the following analyses as recommended by the AWWA and WEF: Long-Term Water Supply And Comprehensive Capacity Analysis; Long-Term Wastewater Treatment And Sewer Capacity Analysis; Financial And Ratepayer Impact Analysis; Infrastructure and Capital Planning Analysis

extraction.summary: The Charter Township of Ypsilanti Board of Trustees adopted Resolution 2026-08 requesting that YCUA impose at least a 12-month moratorium on water and sewer service commitments and approvals for data centers, AI computing facilities, and high-performance computing centers. It calls for AWWA/WEF-based water, wastewater, financial, infrastructure, environmental, and resiliency studies.
extraction.notes: This resolution requests/urges YCUA to impose the moratorium rather than directly enacting a township land-use permitting moratorium.
